question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user enumerate any account's pendingReward by supplying a valid Base32-encoded alias in the GraphQL account query, given that findByAlias returns the full Entity row including staking fields, causing disclosure of unpaid staking reward balances for arbitrary accounts?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user query pendingReward for any account by supplying a known EVM address in the GraphQL account(input: {evmAddress: ...}) query, since findByEvmAddress returns the complete Entity row with no field-level access control on staking reward data, causing full disclosure of unpaid staking rewards for any account?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findById(Long)] Can an unprivileged external user retrieve the pendingReward of any account by querying account(input: {entityId: {num: X}}) for sequential numeric IDs, since findById (inherited from CrudRepository) returns the full Entity including pendingReward with no authorization check, causing mass enumeration of all accounts' unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EntityServiceImpl long-form EVM address encoding (first 4 bytes shard=0, next 8 bytes realm=0, last 8 bytes = entity numeric ID) to call findById instead of findByEvmAddress, bypassing any EVM-address-specific filtering and retrieving pendingReward for accounts that have no EVM address set, causing theft-enabling disclosure of unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SQL condition 'deleted is not true' (which returns TRUE for NULL-deleted rows) in findByAlias to retrieve staking reward data for accounts whose deleted field is NULL, potentially including accounts in an intermediate state where pendingReward has not yet been zeroed out, causing disclosure of rewards that should be inaccessible?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 'deleted is not true' condition in findByEvmAddress (which differs from 'deleted <> true' by including NULL-deleted rows) to retrieve pendingReward for accounts in a soft-deleted or transitional state, causing disclosure of staking rewards that the system considers logically deleted?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user craft a GraphQL query requesting pendingReward with unit=GIGABAR to cause integer overflow or precision loss in the currency conversion, given that pendingReward is a long value in tinybars and the conversion divides by 1,000,000,000, causing the returned value to misrepresent the true pending reward amount and enabling an attacker to exploit the discrepancy?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a GraphQL query with pendingReward(unit: MEGABAR) to cause truncation of the actual tinybar value, given that the conversion loses sub-megabar precision, causing the mirror node to report a lower pending reward than actually accrued and enabling an attacker to identify accounts with sub-megabar rewards that are not visible at coarser units?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly query pendingReward for the staking reward account (0.0.800) via account(input: {entityId: {num: 800}}) to monitor the reserved staking reward pool balance, given that findById returns the full Entity row, causing disclosure of the total unpaid staking rewards reserved in the system and enabling timing attacks on reward distribution?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user observe the stakePeriodStart field returned alongside pendingReward via findByAlias to calculate the exact number of staking periods an account has been accumulating rewards, given that stakePeriodStart is exposed in the GraphQL Account type, causing the attacker to precisely estimate unpaid reward amounts before they are claimed?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user observe the declineReward boolean field returned by findByEvmAddress to identify accounts that are eligible for staking rewards versus those that have opted out, given that declineReward is exposed in the GraphQL Account type, causing the attacker to build a target list of accounts with pending rewards for further exploitation?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findById(Long)] Can an unprivileged external user enumerate all accounts with non-zero pendingReward by iterating over sequential entity IDs via account(input: {entityId: {num: N}}), since findById returns the full Entity row with no rate limiting enforced at the repository layer, causing a complete map of all accounts with unclaimed staking rewards to be built by an attacker?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user supply a malformed or all-zero alias byte array to findByAlias to trigger a full table scan on the entity table, given that the native query uses a parameterized byte array with no length validation at the repository layer, causing a denial-of-service that delays legitimate staking reward queries?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user supply a 20-byte all-zero EVM address to findByEvmAddress, which would match no row via the evm_address column but then fall through to findById(0) in EntityServiceImpl due to the ByteBuffer zero-prefix check, causing an unexpected entity lookup that may return system account staking data?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the GraphQL schema's lack of authentication on the account query to call findByAlias for any known alias and retrieve the stakedNodeId field, given that stakedNodeId is part of the returned Entity, causing disclosure of which node an account is staking to and enabling targeted reward manipulation analysis?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user query the GraphQL API with a crafted EVM address that has shard=0 and realm=0 but a large entity num to probe for high-value staking accounts, since EntityServiceImpl routes such addresses to findById rather than findByEvmAddress, causing the attacker to enumerate accounts by numeric ID while appearing to query by EVM address?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the stale pendingReward data (the schema notes 'there may be delay to reflect the changes in the past staking period') by querying findByAlias repeatedly across staking period boundaries to observe the exact moment pendingReward is updated, causing the attacker to time on-chain transactions to maximize reward theft?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findById(Long)] Can an unprivileged external user query account(input: {entityId: {num: 800}}) to retrieve the staking reward account entity via findById, given that account 0.0.800 is the staking reward treasury and its balance/pendingReward fields are returned in the full Entity row, causing disclosure of the total reserved staking reward pool available for theft?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user use the GraphQL account query with alias input to retrieve the stakedAccountId field from the Entity returned by findByAlias, given that stakedAccountId is part of the Entity domain object, causing disclosure of proxy-staking relationships that reveal which accounts are accumulating delegated staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user craft a GraphQL batch query requesting pendingReward for hundreds of accounts simultaneously via findByEvmAddress, given that there is no per-query rate limiting at the repository layer, causing a high-throughput enumeration of all accounts with pending staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user observe the balance field returned alongside pendingReward by findByAlias to calculate the ratio of pending reward to account balance, given that both fields are returned in the same Entity row, causing the attacker to identify high-reward-to-balance accounts that are most profitable to target for staking reward theft?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findByEvmAddress uses a native SQL query returning 'select *' to receive all entity columns including internal staking metadata (stakedNodeIdStart, stakeTotalStart) that may not be intended for public exposure, causing disclosure of staking state that enables precise reward calculation?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 'select *' native query in findByAlias to receive all columns from the entity table including any future columns added to track staking reward state, given that the query is not column-specific, causing unintended disclosure of new staking-related fields added to the entity table without updating the GraphQL schema?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user submit a GraphQL query requesting the stakedAccount nested field on an Account returned by findByEvmAddress, causing a second findById call to resolve the stakedAccount's pendingReward, enabling the attacker to traverse the staking graph and enumerate rewards for all accounts in a proxy-staking chain?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit GraphQL query nesting to resolve stakedAccount.pendingReward and stakedAccount.stakedAccount.pendingReward recursively via repeated findById calls, given that the Account type has a stakedAccount field of type Account, causing unbounded traversal of the staking graph and mass disclosure of pending rewards?
